question: Q3 - You can use global examples for this question. Each of the examples should however cite a different brand. i) In the context of ingredient co-branding give an example for new attribute expansion ii) In the context of co-branding give an example of modifier and header iii) Give one example for brand communication where celebrities appeared to distract the viewer from the message

answers.brand: Under Armour + Celliant (i) | Missoni for Target (ii) | Johnson Tiles + Katrina Kaif (iii)
answers.brand_concept: (i) Celliant infrared fiber adds energy-recycling attribute to UA RUSH = new attribute expansion from different company. (ii) Missoni=modifier, Target=header — consumer perception driven by Target's price/channel. (iii) Katrina Kaif's glamour overshadowed Johnson Tiles — brand recall nearly negligible.
answers.framework: Keller Ch. 8 (Desai & Keller 2002) | Park, Jun & Shocker | Erfgen et al. 2015 vampire effect
answers.answer_text:
i) Ingredient Co-branding — New Attribute Expansion

Desai & Keller (2002, Keller Ch. 8) distinguish 'slot filler' expansion (changing level of existing attribute, e.g. new scent) from 'new attribute expansion' (adding entirely new characteristic). In ingredient co-branding, the new attribute must come from ANOTHER company.

Under Armour RUSH powered by Celliant (2019): Under Armour, known for moisture-wicking apparel, partnered with Celliant — a separate company and 'the first wellness ingredient brand' specialising in infrared textiles. Celliant's mineral blend is infused into fibers, absorbing body heat and converting it into infrared energy. 'The responsive UA RUSH fabric absorbs that heat and converts it into infrared energy re-emitted back into the body' [1]. This is new attribute expansion: UA historically offered moisture management (existing). Celliant added an entirely NEW attribute — infrared energy recycling for recovery — that UA fabric never possessed. Ingredient from a different company (Hologenix LLC).

ii) Co-branding — Modifier and Header

Park, Jun & Shocker (Keller Ch. 8): In a conceptual combination like 'apartment dog,' apartment=modifier, dog=header. Header brand drives consumer perception.

Missoni for Target (2011): Italian luxury knitwear Missoni partnered with US mass retailer Target. In 'Missoni for Target,' Target is the HEADER — retail platform, pricing ($2.99-$40 range = Target pricing), distribution. Missoni is the MODIFIER — qualifies with luxury design, Italian zigzag prints. Consumer behaviour was driven by header: the launch caused Target's website to crash with 'greater item demand than on a typical Black Friday.' 'The value of the publicity generated was estimated at US$100 million' [2]. Pricing, channel, checkout = all header (Target).

iii) Celebrity Distraction (Vampire Effect)

The 'vampire effect' occurs when a celebrity endorser's presence is so prominent or incongruent that the celebrity 'sucks' attention from the brand. Viewers remember the star, not the brand.

H&R Johnson Tiles featuring Katrina Kaif (2013): Johnson Tiles signed Katrina Kaif as brand ambassador in a multi-crore deal. Classic vampire effect through product-celebrity incongruence: 'The famous Bollywood actress Katrina Kaif was seen endorsing Johnson Tiles. As the relativeness of the celebrity to the product could hardly be understood, the audience could not really get the message. The celebrity was well acknowledged and identified but the brand recall was nearly negligible' [3]. A 'huge shadowing of the celebrity image took over the product leading to a major vampire effect.' Katrina = glamour/dance/cinema endorsing floor tiles = zero cognitive link.

framework_source.section: Science of Branding 8-2: Understanding Brand Alliances (Desai & Keller; Park, Jun & Shocker)
framework_source.cite_as: Keller, Strategic Brand Management, Ch. 8, pp. 304-307
framework_source.page_hint: Lines 934-955 for ingredient branding; Lines 848-871 for modifier-header

citations.quoted_text: The responsive UA RUSH fabric absorbs that heat and converts it into infrared energy that is re-emitted back into the body

citations.author: Divya Negi, Maschanka Fernandes, Raghuram Jeedigunta
citations.title: Impact of Celebrity Endorsements and the Prevalence of Vampire Effect — A Review
citations.publication: Academy of Marketing Studies Journal, Vol. 22, Issue 2

answers.brand: Vibram + Merrell (i) | HGTV Home by Sherwin-Williams (ii) | Pierce Brosnan + Pan Bahar (iii)
answers.brand_concept: (i) Vibram Megagrip soles add premium traction attribute to Merrell boots. (ii) HGTV=modifier, Sherwin-Williams=header. (iii) James Bond persona overshadowed Pan Bahar — brand became footnote in celebrity controversy.
answers.framework: Keller Ch. 8 (Desai & Keller 2002) | Park, Jun & Shocker | Vampire effect
answers.answer_text:
i) Ingredient Co-branding — New Attribute Expansion

Desai & Keller (2002): 'new attribute expansion' = adding entirely new characteristic from a partner company.

Merrell hiking boots with Vibram soles (2007-present): Merrell partnered with Vibram — Italian company, world's leading producer of high-performance rubber soles. Vibram brings proprietary technologies: Megagrip for wet/dry traction, Arctic Grip AT for wet ice, using 'micro logs and performance-tested lug design to provide additional traction' [1]. Before Vibram, Merrell had standard rubber outsoles. Vibram added an entirely NEW attribute — scientifically engineered multi-terrain traction — the boots never possessed. Vibram logo on outsole = visible ingredient branding. Separate company (Vibram S.p.A.).

ii) Modifier and Header

Park, Jun & Shocker: header drives consumer perception; modifier qualifies.

HGTV Home by Sherwin-Williams (2011): Sherwin-Williams (America's largest paint company, 3,300+ stores) partnered with HGTV (home design TV network). Sherwin-Williams = HEADER — paint manufacturer, quality/durability drives perception. HGTV = MODIFIER — adds design inspiration and trend awareness. SVP confirmed: partnership 'capitalises on strengths of both, incorporating HGTV's popularity and design knowledge with Sherwin-Williams' trusted expertise' [2]. Buyers trust header for paint quality; modifier adds curated colour palettes.

iii) Celebrity Distraction (Vampire Effect)

Pan Bahar featuring Pierce Brosnan (2016): Indian pan masala brand signed former James Bond actor. The entire public conversation became about 'James Bond selling pan masala,' not about Pan Bahar. Brosnan later said he was 'deeply shocked and saddened' claiming 'unauthorised and deceptive use' — he'd been told it was a 'breath freshener' [3]. Social media memes focused entirely on the celebrity. Pan Bahar became 'merely a footnote in the celebrity's story.' Devastating vampire effect: brand invested in A-lister to convey 'class' but celebrity persona completely dominated. Ad eventually banned from Indian TV.

citations.author: Vibram
citations.title: Vibram X Merrell collaboration
citations.quoted_text: Traction Lug uses micro logs and performance-tested lug design to provide additional traction

citations.author: PR Newswire / Sherwin-Williams
citations.title: Sherwin-Williams Partners With HGTV
citations.quoted_text: Capitalises on the strengths of both organisations, incorporating HGTV's popularity and design knowledge with Sherwin-Williams' trusted expertise

citations.author: Tara John
citations.title: Pierce Brosnan Apologises for Pan Masala Ad
citations.quoted_text: Brosnan was deeply shocked and saddened after learning his image was used to promote a potentially harmful product

answers.brand: B&O + HP (i) | Fendi x Fila (ii) | Sports betting celebrity saturation (iii)
answers.brand_concept: (i) B&O adds premium audio to HP laptops = new attribute. (ii) Fila=modifier, Fendi=header in luxury streetwear. (iii) Multiple betting brand celebrities — mass vampire effect, no brand recall.
answers.framework: Keller Ch. 8 | Park, Jun & Shocker | Milfeld & Flint 2022
answers.answer_text:
i) Ingredient Co-branding — New Attribute Expansion

HP Inc. partnered with Bang & Olufsen (Danish premium audio company, separate entity) in 2015 after Apple acquired Beats. B&O added: 'dedicated audio island that isolates sensitive audio circuits from other signals on the motherboard,' custom-tuned profiles, proprietary sound algorithms [1]. HP had basic speakers before; B&O added entirely new audiophile-grade acoustic engineering. B&O logo on speaker grille = visible ingredient branding.

ii) Modifier and Header

Fendi Mania — Fendi x Fila (2018): Scottish artist Hey Reilly reinterpreted Fendi's FF logo using Fila's blue-red 'F' lettering. Fendi = HEADER — shown at Milan Fashion Week, Fendi luxury pricing, Fendi distribution. Fila = MODIFIER — sporty 80s/90s streetwear energy qualifies Fendi product. 'When anyone looks to Fila's items, they see Fendi. They see luxury, something special' [2]. Consumer perception driven by header.

iii) Celebrity Distraction (Vampire Effect)

Sports betting industry (2022-2023): Gretzky/McDavid endorsed BetMGM, Neymar endorsed PokerStars, Barkley endorsed FanDuel, Aaron Paul endorsed Bet365. Yet consumers couldn't match celebrity to platform. 'Unless a celebrity spokesperson has a distinct and specific relationship to the brand, customers are much less likely to remember the brand vs. the celebrity' [3]. During 2023 Super Bowl, Milfeld & Flint found: 'star power presents a brand recall problem' — 22% of viewers couldn't name the brand after watching [4]. Saturation of famous faces = mass vampire effect.

citations.title: The Science of Sound: Bang and Olufsen in HP's Devices
citations.quoted_text: A dedicated audio island isolates the sensitive audio circuits from other signals on the motherboard

citations.title: Why Fendi and Fila's Collaboration is Big News
citations.quoted_text: When anyone looks to Fila's items, they see Fendi. They see luxury, something special

citations.author: Mike Barber
citations.title: Beware the Vampire Effect in Advertising
citations.quoted_text: Unless a celebrity spokesperson has a distinct relationship to the brand, customers are much less likely to remember the brand vs. the celebrity

citations.author: Tyler Milfeld, Dan Flint
citations.title: Celebrities Can Overshadow Products in Super Bowl Ads
citations.publication: Haslam College of Business, U. of Tennessee
citations.quoted_text: Star power presents a brand recall problem

answers.brand: Volvo + Bowers & Wilkins (i) | Puma by Alexander McQueen (ii) | Cindy Crawford hair coloring study (iii)
answers.brand_concept: (i) B&W concert-hall audio = new acoustic attribute in Volvo XC90. (ii) McQueen=modifier, Puma=header. (iii) Academic study: Crawford reduced brand recall by 9pp vs unknown model.
answers.framework: Keller Ch. 8 | Park, Jun & Shocker | Erfgen, Zenker & Sattler (2015) IJRM
answers.answer_text:
i) Ingredient Co-branding — New Attribute Expansion

Volvo XC90 with Bowers & Wilkins (2015): B&W (British premium audio company, separate entity) created 1,400-watt, 19-speaker system with 'Gothenburg Concert Hall' setting. Tweeter-on-top technology 'designed to deliver most lifelike sound possible,' Kevlar midrange drivers [1]. This is new attribute expansion: Volvo previously had basic audio. B&W added audiophile-grade concert-hall acoustic engineering — entirely new dimension. B&W logo on speaker grilles = ingredient branding.

ii) Modifier and Header

Puma by Alexander McQueen (2005-2014): In 'PUMA by Alexander McQueen,' Puma = HEADER — sportswear manufacturer, Puma channels, Puma technical platform. McQueen = MODIFIER — avant-garde fashion design adds luxury cachet. 'Laid the groundwork for fashion's acceptance of the bulky or conceptual sneaker' [2]. By 2014 the direction was to 'increase the sports element' — confirming Puma as header driving product category.

iii) Celebrity Distraction (Vampire Effect) — Academic Evidence

Erfgen, Zenker & Sattler (2015, IJRM): Tested two versions of same hair coloring ad with ~1,000 women — one with Cindy Crawford, one with equally attractive unknown model. Results: unaided brand recall 35% with Crawford vs 44% with unknown (9pp decrease). 'It seems participants paid more attention to Cindy than the brand' [3]. Even with seemingly congruent pairing (supermodel + hair product), celebrity fame vampirised brand recall. Most rigorous experimental proof of vampire effect.

NOTE: Puma discussed in class notes (Puma x Ferrari context) — carries mild risk. Volvo NOT on banned list.

citations.author: HomeTheaterHifi.com
citations.title: All-new Volvo XC90 Delivers Top-class B&W Audio
citations.quoted_text: 19 speakers driven by Class D amplifier for 1,400 Watts power

citations.author: Marc Richardson
citations.title: How Puma's Collab with Alexander McQueen Changed Luxury Sneakers
citations.quoted_text: Laid the groundwork for fashion's acceptance of the bulky or conceptual sneaker

citations.author: Utpal Dholakia (citing Erfgen et al. 2015)
citations.title: Can a Celebrity Endorsement Hurt the Brand?
citations.quoted_text: It seems participants paid more attention to Cindy than the brand. Unaided recall 35% vs 44%

chatgpt.response:
i) Ingredient co-branding for **new attribute expansion**  
Ingredient co-branding occurs when a secondary brand provides a specific functional attribute to the host brand. When it enables **new attribute expansion**, the ingredient adds a capability the original product was not strongly associated with.

Examples:

1. **Intel Inside – PC manufacturers (Dell, HP, Lenovo)**  
   - Intel co-brands as an ingredient inside computers.  
   - It expanded the attribute of **processing power and reliability** for otherwise generic PCs.  
   - Consumers began evaluating laptops based on the **Intel processor inside**, adding a new technical-performance attribute to PC branding.  
   - Source: Kotler & Keller; Intel Inside campaign widely documented in Harvard Business Review.

2. **GORE‑TEX in outdoor apparel (Nike, The North Face, Patagonia)**  
   - GORE‑TEX functions as an ingredient brand providing **waterproof and breathable fabric technology**.  
   - When Nike or The North Face add the “GORE‑TEX” label, it expands the product’s attribute set from simply apparel to **high‑performance weather protection**.  
   - Source: W. L. Gore marketing case studies.

3. **Leica camera lenses in Huawei smartphones**  
   - Huawei partnered with **Leica**, a premium German camera brand.  
   - The ingredient branding expanded Huawei phones’ perceived attribute of **professional photography quality**.  
   - This repositioned Huawei devices as photography‑focused smartphones.  
   - Source: Huawei–Leica partnership coverage in TechCrunch and The Verge.

---

ii) Co‑branding example of **Header and Modifier**

In this structure, one brand acts as the **primary (header)** brand while the other acts as a **modifier**, enhancing or signalling a special association.

Examples:

1. **Apple Watch Nike**  
   - **Header brand:** Apple Watch  
   - **Modifier brand:** Nike  
   - Nike modifies the Apple product by adding **sports-focused features, straps, and running integration** with Nike Run Club.

2. **Courtyard by Marriott**  
   - **Header:** Courtyard  
   - **Modifier:** Marriott  
   - Marriott acts as the endorsing modifier to signal **service quality and trust** from the parent hospitality brand.

3. **KitKat by Nestlé**  
   - **Header:** KitKat  
   - **Modifier:** Nestlé  
   - Nestlé acts as an endorsing modifier reinforcing **manufacturing credibility and global food brand trust**.

---

iii) Brand communication where a **celebrity distracted from the core message**

Sometimes celebrity presence becomes so dominant that audiences remember the celebrity more than the brand or message.

Examples:

1. **Pepsi – Kendall Jenner protest ad (2017)**  
   - The ad attempted to portray Pepsi as a symbol of unity in social movements.  
   - However, Kendall Jenner’s presence and the controversial protest imagery overshadowed the product message and led to backlash.  
   - Many viewers remembered the controversy rather than the brand communication.  
   - Source: BBC, New York Times coverage of the Pepsi ad controversy.

2. **Shopee – Cristiano Ronaldo campaign (2021 Southeast Asia)**  
   - Ronaldo appeared dancing to Shopee’s jingle in a viral ad.  
   - Audiences focused heavily on Ronaldo’s unexpected dancing rather than Shopee’s marketplace value proposition.  
   - The celebrity spectacle dominated recall of the advertisement.

3. **Thums Up ads with Salman Khan (India)**  
   - The ads heavily emphasized Salman Khan’s stunt persona.  
   - In many campaigns, the audience recall centered on **Salman Khan’s action sequences** more than the specific brand message about the beverage itself.  
   - Source: Indian advertising commentary in Economic Times Brand Equity.

These examples illustrate how celebrity power can sometimes **overshadow brand communication**, reducing clarity of the intended marketing message.
